title: Pig heat detection and natural mating
subtitle: Reading the sow's cycle and managing the boar meeting.
excerpt: A sow comes into heat every 21 days and is receptive for 2 to 3 days. Missing the heat costs a full cycle; catching it at the right point and managing the boar meeting safely produces a litter of 8 to 12 piglets 116 days later. Heat detection is a learned skill that rewards daily observation.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs (2003). AHDB Pork, breeding and reproduction factsheets, ahdb.org.uk. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg, historical reference on smallholding pig management.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fertile period in a sow's cycle when she is receptive to the boar and ovulation is imminent. In pigs the cycle is 21 days; oestrus lasts 2 to 3 days. A sow in oestrus shows standing reflex, vulval swelling, and a characteristic immobile posture when back pressure is applied.

content.text: accurately means timing the boar meeting for maximum fertility. A sow mated on day one of a three-day heat cycle will conceive; one mated two days before the heat starts will not. Daily observation in the weeks leading up to the expected heat makes detection straightforward.
Swollen, reddened vulva in the 24 hours before the standing heat begins
Restless behaviour, reduced feed intake, and interest in the boar's scent through a dividing fence

The standing reflex: when back pressure is applied (place both hands on the loin region and press firmly), a sow in peak oestrus stands immobile with her ears erect. A sow that moves away is not yet in standing heat.

The most reliable heat-detection tool is the boar himself. Walking a boar along the outside of a sow pen once daily causes sows in oestrus to stand at the fence. If you do not have access to a boar, recorded boar vocalisations and boar odour (pheromone aerosols are available from agricultural suppliers) simulate the stimulus for the standing reflex test.

Introduce the boar to the sow's pen rather than the other way around, a sow on unfamiliar territory is more anxious. A maiden gilt may need more than one attempt before she accepts mounting. Two matings 12 to 24 hours apart maximise conception rate. Watch the first mating from a distance and be ready to separate the animals if the boar is aggressive or the sow is clearly refusing. A single firm mounting lasting 3 to 5 minutes is a successful service.

Return the boar to his own pen after mating. Check the sow for returning to oestrus 21 days later, a sow that returns to heat was not successfully mated or reabsorbed the litter. If no return is seen at 21 days, the sow is likely in pig; confirm visually by week 6 when abdominal swelling begins.
